authoritative_sources.title: 14 CFR Part 139 - Certification of Airports
authoritative_sources.source_class: Transportation, customs, or safety regulator guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Part 139 is the federal airport certification rule set for covered U.S. airports and defines the airport certification manual, operational safety, inspection, rescue, wildlife, marking, lighting, condition, and record expectations that constrain airport operations work.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: airport operations intake, certification-manual dependency review, safety-condition gating, and completion evidence control
used_for: airport certification and operational safety boundary

authoritative_sources.title: 14 CFR 139.327 - Self-inspection program
authoritative_sources.source_class: Transportation, customs, or safety regulator guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: This section directly governs airport self-inspection programs and records, making it authoritative for airfield inspection evidence, corrective-action tracking, and refusal to close unsafe or undocumented work.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: airfield inspection evidence review, defect handling, corrective-action tracking, and closure control

authoritative_sources.title: AC 150/5200-18 - Airport Safety Self-Inspection
authoritative_sources.source_class: Transportation, customs, or safety regulator guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: FAA airport safety guidance explains self-inspection practices and evidence expectations used by airport operators to identify, document, correct, and follow up on unsafe or nonconforming airport conditions.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: self-inspection intake, defect documentation, exception rework, and closure evidence capture

authoritative_sources.title: AC 150/5200-30D - Airport Field Condition Assessments and Winter Operations Safety
authoritative_sources.source_class: Equipment, route, or service-level operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: FAA guidance defines field condition assessment and winter operations safety practices that affect route, runway, taxiway, ramp, braking, snow, ice, condition-code, and operational-notification handling.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: field condition assessment, winter operations disruption handling, route-impact packaging, and NOTAM dependency review
used_for: snow and ice disruption scenario design

authoritative_sources.title: AC 150/5210-20A - Ground Vehicle Operations to include Taxiing or Towing an Aircraft on Airports
authoritative_sources.source_class: Equipment, route, or service-level operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: FAA airport safety guidance addresses ground vehicle, pedestrian, taxiing, towing, movement area, non-movement area, apron, and runway safety area controls that constrain airport operations movement and equipment summaries.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: movement-area dependency review, apron and vehicle exception handling, equipment or route blocker escalation
used_for: AOA, apron, movement-area, and vehicle-control terminology

authoritative_sources.title: FAA Air Traffic Publications - Airport Conditions
authoritative_sources.source_class: Chain-of-custody, dispatch, or movement workflow references
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: FAA air traffic publications describe airport condition handling and NOTAM-relevant unsafe conditions, making them authoritative for runway, lighting, obstruction, braking, closure, and notification boundaries that airport operations must not bypass.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: airport condition reporting, NOTAM dependency handling, unsafe-condition escalation, and route-status update packaging

authoritative_sources.title: 49 CFR Part 1542 - Airport Security
authoritative_sources.source_class: Transportation, customs, or safety regulator guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Part 1542 governs U.S. airport operator security programs, access controls, security areas, and security response boundaries, so airport operations must route rather than decide security-sensitive work.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: security-sensitive exception handling, AOA or sterile-area boundary review, and adjacent-owner routing
used_for: refusal triggers for access or security determinations

authoritative_sources.title: AMS Air Features
authoritative_sources.source_class: Transportation, customs, or safety regulator guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CBP is the U.S. customs authority, and this Air AMS guidance explains advance electronic air cargo information expectations under CBP regulations, defining customs documentation boundaries for airport cargo movement.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: air cargo documentation gap review, customs-owner routing, and movement-blocker escalation
used_for: air cargo manifest and house shipment evidence handling

authoritative_sources.title: 19 CFR 122.48a - Electronic information for air cargo required in advance of arrival
authoritative_sources.source_class: Transportation, customs, or safety regulator guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: This customs regulation defines advance electronic air cargo information requirements for inbound air cargo, supporting escalation when airport operations is asked to clear or release customs-dependent cargo movement.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: air cargo documentation prerequisite validation, customs blocker handling, and adjacent customs/compliance routing

authoritative_sources.title: IATA Baggage Tracking - Resolution 753
authoritative_sources.source_class: Chain-of-custody, dispatch, or movement workflow references
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: IATA Resolution 753 defines baggage tracking points and audit-trail expectations used by airlines, airports, and handlers to establish acquisition, load, transfer, and arrival custody visibility.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: baggage custody review, transfer exception handling, handoff evidence capture, and passenger-impact escalation
used_for: handoff evidence and missing scan failure mode

authoritative_sources.title: IATA Ground Operations Manual
authoritative_sources.source_class: Equipment, route, or service-level operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: IATA ground operations standards are recognized airline and ground-handler operating references for aircraft turnaround, load control interfaces, ramp safety, service sequencing, and ground handling terminology.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: turnaround-readiness execution, equipment dependency mapping, service-level exception handling, and handoff closure control

workflow_stages.name: Accepted intake and authority classification
workflow_stages.description: Normalize the requested airport operations outcome, facility or movement context, affected flight, stand, gate, belt, route, custody, equipment, and documentation records, then classify the work as in-scope, out-of-scope, or blocked before execution progresses.
source_classes: Transportation, customs, or safety regulator guidance
Chain-of-custody, dispatch, or movement workflow references

workflow_stages.name: Prerequisite and system-of-record validation
workflow_stages.description: Validate airport operations logs, airfield inspections, condition assessments, NOTAM references, gate or stand records, baggage and cargo custody records, equipment status, maintenance status, customs documentation, security constraints, and tenant SOP context.
source_classes: Transportation, customs, or safety regulator guidance
Equipment, route, or service-level operating standards

workflow_stages.name: Lifecycle progression and movement or turnaround execution
workflow_stages.description: Move accepted work through accepted, in-progress, evidence-reviewed, blocked, rework-pending, escalated, completed, or returned states only when required records, owners, and dependency status support the transition.
source_classes: Chain-of-custody, dispatch, or movement workflow references
Equipment, route, or service-level operating standards

workflow_stages.name: Exception queue and irregular-operations handling
workflow_stages.description: Capture equipment faults, field-condition changes, route or schedule disruption, custody breaks, missing documentation, passenger-impact issues, and unsafe-condition signals as exception summaries with next owners and rework criteria.
source_classes: Transportation, customs, or safety regulator guidance
Chain-of-custody, dispatch, or movement workflow references
Equipment, route, or service-level operating standards

workflow_stages.name: Adjacent authority routing
workflow_stages.description: Route dispatch release, ATC clearance, customs release, TSA or airport security, maintenance return-to-service, emergency response, customer recovery, warehouse, and safety-critical decisions to the correct owner instead of resolving them inside airport operations.
source_classes: Transportation, customs, or safety regulator guidance

workflow_stages.name: Completion evidence and audit-ready closure
workflow_stages.description: Confirm closure only when lifecycle state, source-system evidence, handoff or destination-owner acknowledgement, blocker resolution, and residual risk posture are documented; otherwise refuse completion and keep the state reversible.
source_classes: Transportation, customs, or safety regulator guidance
Chain-of-custody, dispatch, or movement workflow references

artifacts: airport operations log entries, AOC records, shift briefings, station records, and irregular-operations tickets
flight, tail, gate, stand, hardstand, belt, bay, route, taxiway, runway, and service-level records
Part 139 inspection records, field-condition assessments, RWYCC records, FOD sweep logs, corrective-action notes, closure notices, and NOTAM references
turnaround records, load records, ULD records, baggage scans, BHS/BRS records, baggage reconciliation, cargo manifest, and chain-of-custody handoff records
equipment status, GSE status, passenger boarding bridge, PCA, GPU, belt, deicing, fueling, lighting, pavement, wildlife, snow or ice, facility, and service logs

maintenance work orders, return-to-service references, TSA or airport security program constraints, CBP air cargo documentation, customs references, passenger-impact notes, and customer operations records
systems_of_record: airport operations center or airport operations log
airport certification manual and airport rules repository
FIDS, AODB, gate management, stand management, and resource management systems
airfield inspection and field-condition reporting system
FAA NOTAM system or local NOTAM workflow reference
BHS, BRS, baggage reconciliation, and ULD tracking systems
cargo booking, manifest, and customs documentation systems
telematics, fleet, GSE, and equipment-status systems
security access-control and airport security program systems
customer operations, CRM, and service-desk systems

allowed: Classify airport operations requests as in scope, out of scope, or blocked on missing prerequisites.
Advance, hold, rework, escalate, return, or complete work based on documented lifecycle state and evidence.
Produce execution status updates, exception summaries, movement or turnaround summaries, chain-of-custody notes, irregular-operations escalation notes, and completion evidence records.
Route work to dispatch, customs, security, maintenance, warehouse, ground handling, customer operations, airport compliance, or human reviewers when ownership shifts.
escalate: Airfield condition, NOTAM, equipment, maintenance, customs, security, custody, service-level, passenger-impact, or handoff evidence is missing or materially conflicts.
Movement depends on dispatch release, ATC clearance, customs release, TSA or airport security determination, maintenance return-to-service, emergency response, or safety-critical approval.
Public baseline sources, airport certification manual, local airport rules, tenant SOPs, airline SLAs, or jurisdiction-specific rules materially conflict.
Owner mapping is ambiguous across airport operations, airline, ground handler, warehouse, maintenance, security, customs, or customer operations teams.
refuse: Issue or imply flight dispatch release, ATC clearance, customs release, TSA security determination, maintenance return-to-service approval, legal interpretation, financial approval, emergency command, or safety signoff.
Mark work complete without required source-system evidence, handoff or destination-owner acknowledgement, and blocker resolution.
Invent custody proof, condition status, documentation status, route status, equipment status, or completion evidence not present in authoritative records.
Bypass safety, security, customs, maintenance, dispatch, or condition-reporting controls to preserve a schedule or service-level target.

domain_failure_modes: movement or turnaround work is marked complete without baggage, cargo, gate, stand, equipment, handoff, or destination-owner evidence
airport condition or NOTAM-relevant information is treated as routine status instead of a safety or route-impact escalation
baggage, cargo, or ULD custody breaks are hidden by generic completion notes instead of missing-scan or handoff exception records
snow, ice, lighting, pavement, wildlife, FOD, contaminated-stand, or field-condition issues are advanced without safety-critical owner review
customs, security, dispatch, ATC, or maintenance authority is implied by airport operations because a queue owner wants fast closure
airline service-level pressure causes the lane to promise departure, gate, customs, or safety outcomes outside its authority
systems of record disagree on flight, stand, gate, belt, equipment, route, or documentation status and the conflict is not escalated
tenant-local shortcuts override FAA, TSA, CBP, IATA, airport certification manual, or local airport-rule evidence requirements

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Route or schedule disruption with compliance impact
Request that implies customs, dispatch, or safety authority

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Airport-specific certification manual content, airport rules, airline SLAs, ground-handler SOPs, security program details, customs procedures, emergency plans, local labor rules, and owner maps vary materially by tenant and facility.
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: This pack uses FAA, eCFR, TSA, CBP, and IATA sources as the public baseline. Runtime must retrieve tenant facility, jurisdiction, service model, system-of-record, owner map, airport certification manual, airport rules, and airline or handler agreements before applying tenant-specific outcomes.

notes: The research baseline covers workflow, evidence, terminology, movement and custody handling, safety and customs boundaries, equipment and route implications, and failure behavior rather than only generic airport labels.

The lane stops at airport operations execution support. Dispatch release, customs release, TSA or airport security determinations, ATC clearance, maintenance return-to-service, legal interpretation, emergency command, and safety signoff remain outside the lane even when operational pressure is high.
